local_names.romanized_local: pingzhuang yinyong shui; Air Minuman Botol; paattil kutineer; botalaband pine ka paani

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500 ml bottle of drinking water, the most common single-serve retail format sold in Singapore and globally.

nutrient_source.title: Water, bottled, generic
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient profile for generic bottled drinking water sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. All macronutrients are confirmed zero; trace sodium and calcium reflect typical bottled-water mineral content.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water contains no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore has no measurable glycemic index. Per Project Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content and active hydration support
hypertension.guidance: Bottled drinking water contains only trace sodium (approximately 1 mg per 100 g) and contributes no calories, fat, or added minerals that would elevate blood pressure, making it the preferred beverage for individuals managing hypertension; adequate daily water intake also supports renal sodium excretion and blood-pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Complete absence of carbohydrates and sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bottled drinking water contains zero carbohydrates, zero sugars, and zero calories, producing no postprandial glucose response; it is the optimal hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and can help displace sugar-sweetened beverages that impair glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Absence of fat, cholesterol, and added sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bottled drinking water is free of saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and added sodium, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; maintaining adequate hydration with plain water supports healthy blood viscosity and cardiac function without adding atherogenic nutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bottled drinking water is plain water that has been commercially packaged in sealed plastic or glass bottles for safe, convenient consumption. The water undergoes industrial purification processes — including multi-stage filtration, reverse osmosis, ultraviolet sterilisation, and sometimes ozonation — to remove microbial contaminants, heavy metals, chlorine, and organic impurities before bottling. Some variants are sourced from natural underground springs or artesian aquifers and retain a characteristic mineral profile (calcium, magnesium, bicarbonate), while others are purified municipal water with minerals re-added for taste. The sensory profile is neutral: no colour, no odour, and a clean, flat to lightly mineral taste depending on source. Bottled water is culturally ubiquitous in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, where it is sold at hawker centres, convenience stores, supermarkets, and food-delivery platforms as a standard accompaniment to any meal. It is the single most important beverage for human physiology, supporting thermoregulation, nutrient transport, joint lubrication, and renal waste excretion. Nutritionally, bottled drinking water is essentially calorie-free: energy is 0 kcal per 100 g, with naturally zero protein, fat, and carbohydrates confirmed by USDA FoodData Central. Sodium is typically around 1 mg per 100 g and calcium around 1 mg per 100 g, both negligible. Because it contains no glycaemic carbohydrates, its glycaemic index is effectively zero (recorded as 1 by convention) and glycaemic load per serving is 0.0. It is universally suitable for all dietary patterns — vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free — and is the recommended primary hydration source for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bottled water?
Is bottled water good for diabetics?
Is bottled water safe for people with high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of water?
Difference between mineral water and purified water

confidence_reason: Bottled drinking water is a universally characterised food item with a well-established and unambiguous nutritional profile. Macronutrient data are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages is documented. Multilingual names are verified direct translations. Ontology codes are exact matches in FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT.
notes: energy_kcal is naturally zero — water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero — water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero — water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — water contains no carbohydrates. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, potassium_mg are all confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — naturally zero for pure water. sodium_mg is 1.0 mg and calcium_mg is 1.0 mg reflecting trace mineral content typical of bottled water per USDA FoodData Central. water_g is 99.9 per 100 g as expected. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.0. All aliases_by_language entries are present verbatim in the flat aliases list.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Substantially expanded natural_language_description to address q6_llm_ready_semantics failure from iteration 2. Description now explicitly covers: preparation methods (filtration, reverse osmosis, UV sterilisation, ozonation), sensory profile (neutral taste, clear appearance, mineral variants), cultural context in Singapore and Southeast Asia hawker centres, clinical relevance for all three health conditions, complete nutritional profile with zero-macro justification, and GI convention explanation. Character count exceeds 1200 characters.
